metadata.working_title: Draphnet: Integrating drug bioassays and disease genetics to map drug impacts on the human phenome

abstract_sketch.problem: Many drugs have unintended effects on human phenotypes, creating both safety concerns and drug-repurposing opportunities, yet the biological basis of these effects is rarely known.
abstract_sketch.gap: Existing computational methods predict drug-phenotype relationships but do not jointly learn how drug molecular effects propagate through disease driver genes to produce phenotypic effects.
abstract_sketch.approach: We develop Draphnet, a supervised logistic affinity-regression model trained on 429 compounds, 1391 ToxCast endpoints, 10,027 PhenomeXcan disease genes, and 197 UK Biobank phenotypes, that learns a shared drug-endpoint-to-disease-gene network.
abstract_sketch.key_results: Drug molecular similarity tracks phenotypic similarity (p=1e-22; linear model p=1.7e-43); held-out side-effect predictions beat a nearest-neighbor baseline (rank-sum p=3e-8); 28 of 132 DrugBank targets have significantly associated disease genes (adjusted p<0.01), including HTR2C->PPM1M and PPARalpha->CETP.
abstract_sketch.impact: Draphnet provides an interpretable, biology-anchored view of drug effects that can generate mechanistic hypotheses for repurposing and personalized medicine.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: Unintended drug effects are common and of major public-health importance; repurposing opportunities motivate systematic approaches.
Existing data resources (SIDER, DrugBank, Drug Repurposing Hub) catalog drug-phenotype annotations, while ToxCast/Tox21 and PhenomeXcan provide molecular and genetic profiles.
Prior computational approaches (connectivity score; S-PrediXcan-based drug-disease contrast; matrix completion) focus on prediction, not biological interpretation.
Gap: no method has jointly integrated bioassay profiles with phenome-wide disease genetics in an interpretable model.
We propose Draphnet, which learns a shared network W from endpoints to disease genes across thousands of drug-phenotype pairs, enabling both prediction and mechanistic interpretation.

subsections.title: Drug molecular similarity predicts phenotypic similarity
content_bullets: Compile D (429 compounds x 1391 endpoints) with ~100 endpoints per drug; use SoftImpute to obtain U_D S_D.
Drugs with similar ToxCast profiles share more SIDER phenotypes (Spearman p=1e-22; linear model adjusted for shared-endpoint count p=1.7e-43).
Diseases with more similar PhenomeXcan profiles share more drugs (p=4e-81; Spearman = -0.11, Fig 1C).
Similarity is preserved after dimensionality reduction (Spearman = 0.21 between D-space and U_D S_D-space similarity).

subsections.title: Draphnet generalizes to held-out drugs and outperforms a nearest-neighbor baseline
content_bullets: Logistic affinity regression over (V_P otimes U_D S_D) * stack(W_DP) = logit(p(Y)).
Selected hyperparameters: r_P=131, r_D=95, lasso regularization=1.
20-fold CV with 5% of drugs held out per fold; Draphnet Jaccard distance < nearest neighbor for majority of drugs (rank-sum p=3e-8, Fig 2A).
Qualitative top predictions include fludrocortisone-eczema, methyclothiazide-glaucoma, and dasatinib-cancers (despite leukemia being absent from P).

subsections.title: The drug phenome-effect matrix recapitulates known drug biology
content_bullets: Drugs sharing >=1 known target have more similar ToxCast endpoint effects (rank-sum p=1e-28).
True projection distinguishes drugs sharing targets better than a permutation null for most targets (Fig 2B).
Within pairs sharing a target, phenome-effect Spearman correlation is systematically higher in true vs null projection (Fig 2C; stars denote significance thresholds, including p<1e-5).
CHRM1 exemplifies polypharmacology-driven exceptions (14 drugs, median 19 other targets).

subsections.title: Mapping drugs onto the disease genome reveals shared-target disease genes
content_bullets: Project each drug into 10,027-gene disease-genome space via inverse SVD of P (equation 2).
Significance by empirical null (10,000 Y permutations), Benjamini-Yekutieli-adjusted per drug.
Median 7 disease genes per drug; 28 of 132 DrugBank targets (shared by >=3 drugs) show disease-gene enrichments at adj. p<0.01 (Fig 3A calibration).
t-SNE of drug target significance clusters by ATC category (Fig 3B).

Notable associations: PPM1M with HTR2C-targeting neuroleptics (top PhenomeXcan gene for bipolar disorder); CETP with fenofibrate and PPARalpha-targeting drugs (prior experimental support as fenofibrate/PPARalpha effector).

subsections.title: A novel drug categorization from disease-gene cliques
content_bullets: Construct a drug-drug graph connecting pairs with more shared disease genes than a weighted random null (1,000 simulations); extract maximal cliques with networkx.
Recovered cliques include anti-inflammatories + PPARalpha-agonists + pyridoxine; calcium channel blockers (bepridil, verapamil) + pimozide; mecamylamine + pindolol + gabapentin.
Visualize as bipartite drug-gene graph with igraph (Fig 4); keep only disease genes associated with >=1 target and <15 drugs; only cliques with >=3 drugs.

sections.title: Methods
content_bullets: PhenomeXcan construction: |Phi^-1(multiXcan_p)| * consensus S-PrediXcan sign across tissues; keep top-half of most-variable genes (10,027); match to SIDER via UMLS CUIs.
ToxCast Level 5 matrix D (429 x 1391) with ~100 endpoints per drug; SoftImpute with CV-like masking of 5% non-missing entries for rank/regularization choice.
Affinity regression reformulation via P = U_P S_P V_P^T and Kronecker product; L1-regularized logistic regression; tuning via 10% drug hold-out per fold.
Phenome-effect matrix U_D S_D W_DP; disease-genome matrix via equation 2 using inverses of the SVD of P.
Permutation null: 10,000 Y permutations, BY-adjusted empirical p-values per drug-gene; target-enrichment hypergeometric tests with BH correction.
Drug-drug graph construction for cliques: weighted-random disease-gene null with 1,000 simulations; maximal cliques via networkx; bipartite visualization via igraph.

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: Restate: Draphnet integrates two untapped data modalities (ToxCast, PhenomeXcan) into a shared, interpretable model of drug effects on disease.

Prior work either predicts without interpretation (connectivity score, matrix completion) or uses gene expression for narrow disease families (So et al.); we generalize and enrich with genetic drivers.
Interpretability lets us suggest disease-gene mechanisms for unexpected effects (e.g., PPM1M for HTR2C-modulating neuroleptics; CETP for fenofibrate).
Limitations: training-set size relative to parameter count, ambiguity of GWAS-to-gene mapping (example: RP11-54O7.17 vs PERM1), incompleteness of SIDER (latent positives appearing as negatives).

Future directions: integration of LINCS Connectivity Map, personalized-risk extensions using individual-level allele profiles, pathway-level projections, analysis of shared pathways across unexpected disease groupings.

figure_plan.caption: Draphnet connects drug endpoints to disease genes to phenotypes through a shared learned network.
Draphnet achieves lower Jaccard distance than nearest neighbor for predicted side-effect profiles (rank-sum p=3e-8).
Fraction of DrugBank targets (of 132) with disease-gene enrichments at adjusted p<0.01.
Selected drug-disease-gene associations including PPM1M/HTR2C and CETP/PPARalpha. Full data in Supplementary Table 2.
